description:
You are acting as a monitoring-platform specialist who has been asked to deliver a short proof-of-concept alerting demo.  
Everything must live inside the non-privileged user’s home directory (/home/user) and **no sudo must be used**.  
Follow the checklist exactly – the automated grader will look for the precise files, paths and contents described below.  

CHECKLIST  
1. Create the directory tree  
   /home/user/sys_monitoring/alerts  
   (All parent directories must exist.  Default permissions created by “mkdir –p” are fine.)  

2. Create a single text configuration file  
   /home/user/sys_monitoring/thresholds.conf  
   The file must contain exactly the two lines shown here, in this order, ending with a newline character after the second line:  
   CPU_LOAD_MAX=2.00  
   MEM_USED_MAX=85  
   (Do not add spaces, comments or extra blank lines.)  

3. Produce an executable Bash script named  
   /home/user/sys_monitoring/monitor_demo.sh  
   • It must start with the normal she-bang (#!/usr/bin/env bash).  
   • When executed with no arguments it must write two fixed, pre-canned alert messages to STDOUT *and* append the same two messages to  
     /home/user/sys_monitoring/alerts/alert.log  
   • The script must read the two numeric threshold values from /home/user/sys_monitoring/thresholds.conf (use “source” or a similar mechanism)  
     but it does **not** have to calculate real CPU or memory usage – this is only a demo.  
   • The two alert lines your script prints / appends must be **exactly** the following (trailing whitespace is **not** allowed).  
     The timestamp portion is fixed; do not generate the current date – hard-code the string exactly as shown:  
     [2023-04-01 12:00:00] ALERT: CPU load 1min exceeded threshold (value=5.50 threshold=2.00)  
     [2023-04-01 12:00:00] ALERT: Memory usage exceeded threshold (value=95% threshold=85%)  

4. Make the script executable (chmod 755).  

5. Run the script once so that /home/user/sys_monitoring/alerts/alert.log exists at the end of the task and contains the two alert lines above, in the order shown, each line terminated by a single newline character and **nothing else** (no header, no blank lines).  

EXPECTED FILE SUMMARY (after you have finished):  
• /home/user/sys_monitoring/thresholds.conf         – exactly 2 lines as described.  
• /home/user/sys_monitoring/monitor_demo.sh         – executable Bash script.  
• /home/user/sys_monitoring/alerts/alert.log        – exactly the 2 fixed alert lines shown.  

Do not print the contents of these files to the console unless you are verifying your own work; only their presence and correctness are graded.
truth:
/home/user/sys_monitoring/thresholds.conf  
-----------------------------------------  
CPU_LOAD_MAX=2.00  
MEM_USED_MAX=85  
<newline at end of file>

/home/user/sys_monitoring/alerts/alert.log  
------------------------------------------  
[2023-04-01 12:00:00] ALERT: CPU load 1min exceeded threshold (value=5.50 threshold=2.00)  
[2023-04-01 12:00:00] ALERT: Memory usage exceeded threshold (value=95% threshold=85%)  
<newline at end of file>

# The test harness will also check that /home/user/sys_monitoring/monitor_demo.sh:
#   • exists,
#   • is executable (mode 755 or similar),
#   • contains a she-bang line “#!/usr/bin/env bash” as its first line,
#   • sources /home/user/sys_monitoring/thresholds.conf,
#   • appends (not overwrites) the two exact alert lines above to the log file when run.
name: task_000000_e04c3634
